sections.header: Verses 1–7 — "God Sent His Son, Born under the Law"

heb.paragraph: A distinctively Pauline term (Rom 8:15, 23; 9:4; Eph 1:5) denoting the legal act by which a person is brought into a family with full rights of inheritance. In Roman law, adoption conferred the same status as biological sonship. Believers’ status as God’s children is not natural but gracious—an act of divine initiative.

One of the most debated expressions in Galatians. It may refer to basic principles of religious knowledge, elemental spirits, or the physical elements. Paul applies the term to both Jews (under the law) and Gentiles (under paganism) before Christ, suggesting that both systems function as enslavement to sub-Christian spiritual realities.

refs.note: Paul’s fuller exposition of adoption parallels Gal 4:4–7: those led by the Spirit are God’s children, crying ‘Abba, Father,’ and are co-heirs with Christ.
The incarnational language—‘the Word became flesh’—provides the Johannine parallel to Paul’s compressed Christology in Gal 4:4.

notes.note: Being under the law was a form of minority and slavery, not because the law was evil but because it could not confer mature sonship. The stoicheia reduce both Jews and Gentiles to the same condition of bondage.

The incarnation is described with deliberate brevity and maximal theological content. ‘God sent his Son’ implies pre-existence; ‘born of a woman’ affirms genuine humanity; ‘born under the law’ places Christ within the covenant framework he came to fulfil. The purpose clauses define salvation: liberation from bondage and entrance into sonship.

The Spirit’s cry of ‘Abba, Father’ is the experiential proof of adoption. The trinitarian structure: the Father sends the Son; the Son redeems; the Father sends the Spirit of the Son into believers’ hearts.

Calvin marvels at the economy of Paul’s language: in two verses he encompasses eternal generation, incarnation, law-satisfaction, redemption, and adoption. ‘Born of a woman’ affirms genuine human nature against docetism; ‘born under the law’ means voluntary submission to the law’s authority in order to bear its penalty.

The Aramaic ‘Abba’ reflects the intimacy of the filial relationship. Slaves address their master by title; only children cry ‘Father.’ The Spirit’s testimony in the believer’s heart is the internal seal of an external legal act.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase to plērōma tou chronou denotes the divinely appointed moment. The NET translators note that ‘born of a woman’ uses ginomai rather than gennaō, emphasising mode of existence rather than biological process.

The double designation ‘Abba, ho patēr’ preserves the Aramaic term Jesus himself used in prayer (Mark 14:36). The verb exapesteilen draws a parallel between the sending of the Son and the sending of the Spirit.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Galatians, BECNT (2013) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo identifies this passage as the christological centre of Galatians. The stoicheia are best understood as basic religious principles governing both Jewish and Gentile existence before Christ. Moo notes the chiastic structure of vv. 4–5: God sent his Son (A) / born of a woman, born under the law (B) / to redeem those under the law (B′) / that we might receive adoption (A′).

bruce.source: F.F. Bruce, Galatians, NIGTC (1982) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Bruce notes the inheritance analogy draws on both Jewish and Greco-Roman legal conventions. In Roman law, the father set the date when the minor would assume full control. The cry ‘Abba, Father’ preserves the ipsissima vox of Jesus and signifies that believers share in the Son’s own relationship with the Father.

hist.context: Paul develops the paidagogos metaphor into a fuller analogy of inheritance. An heir who is still a minor is no different from a slave, even though he owns the whole estate (v. 1). Before Christ, God’s people were ‘enslaved under the elemental spiritual forces’ (v. 3). But when ‘the fullness of time’ arrived, God sent his Son, born of a woman, born under the law, to redeem those under the law, that they might receive adoption (vv. 4–5). The Christological affirmation in vv. 4–5 affirms the pre-existence of the Son, his incarnation, his submission to the law, his redemptive work, and the consequent adoption of believers.

sections.header: Verses 8–20 — "How Can You Turn Back to Slavery?"

heb.paragraph: Paul describes the stoicheia as ‘weak and miserable’ (asthenē kai ptōcha, v. 9). The adjectives denote impotence: the elemental forces lack the power to bring about the righteousness they demand. To return to them is a step backward from power to impotence.

refs.note: Paul’s warning against judging regarding festivals, New Moons, or Sabbath days parallels Gal 4:10. Both treat calendar observance as a return to the shadow superseded by Christ.
Paul’s tender self-description as a nursing mother parallels the parental imagery of Gal 4:19.

notes.note: The equation of Torah observance with pagan bondage is radical. Both systems, when relied upon for justification, function as forms of slavery to the stoicheia. The observance of ‘days and months’ refers to the entire Jewish liturgical system the agitators are imposing.

Paul’s emotional appeal reveals the heart of a pastor watching his spiritual children walk away. The agitators’ strategy is classic manipulation: isolate converts from their founding pastor, then redirect their loyalty. Paul’s response is parental: ‘I am again in the pains of childbirth until Christ is formed in you’ (v. 19).

Calvin explains that the ‘weak and miserable forces’ include both pagan idolatry and Mosaic ceremonies when observed as necessary for salvation. After sunrise, to light a candle is not wrong but foolish.

The birth-pangs metaphor reveals pastoral suffering. Paul had already given birth to the Galatians through his initial preaching; now he must endure the agony again because the agitators have undone his work.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb epistrepheō implies reversal: the Galatians are regressing. The phrase palin anōthen intensifies the regression: they are starting over in slavery.
The phrase di’ astheneian tēs sarkos has generated numerous theories about Paul’s ailment: malaria, epilepsy, ophthalmia, or a disfiguring skin condition.

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Galatians, BECNT (2013) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo reads the passage as a pastoral intensification. The equation of Torah observance with pagan bondage is structural: both systems, apart from Christ, function as sub-Christian regimes. Paul’s emotional appeal reveals that the theological dispute has a relational dimension.

bruce.source: F.F. Bruce, Galatians, NIGTC (1982) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Bruce notes the paradox of v. 9: the Galatians came to know God, but more importantly, were known by God—the initiative is divine. Bruce suggests Paul’s illness may have been malaria contracted in Pamphylia, prompting his journey to the higher elevations of Galatian Phrygia.

hist.context: Paul shifts from theology to pastoral appeal. Before conversion the Galatians were enslaved to beings that ‘by nature are not gods’ (v. 8). Now they are turning back to ‘weak and miserable forces,’ observing ‘special days and months and seasons and years’ (v. 10)—the Jewish liturgical calendar the agitators are imposing. Paul fears his labour has been ‘wasted’ (v. 11). The emotional intensity increases in vv. 12–20: Paul appeals to their former love, recalling how they received him despite his illness (v. 13) and would have ‘torn out your eyes’ for him (v. 15). He contrasts his own motives with the agitators’ desire to ‘alienate’ the Galatians (v. 17).

sections.header: Verses 21–31 — "The Allegory of Hagar and Sarah"

heb.paragraph: The verb appears only here in the NT. Paul explicitly identifies his interpretive method: the Genesis narrative is read typologically, with Hagar = Sinai covenant = slavery = present Jerusalem and Sarah = promise covenant = freedom = Jerusalem above. This is not arbitrary allegory but typological correspondence grounded in historical pattern.

refs.note: The original narrative of Ishmael’s expulsion provides the historical basis for Paul’s typological application.
Paul quotes Isaiah’s barren-woman oracle in v. 27, applying it to the Jerusalem above: the barren Sarah will have more children than Hagar.
The contrast between earthly Sinai and heavenly Jerusalem uses similar spatial theology.

notes.note: Paul uses the Judaizers’ own weapon against them. Those born ‘according to the flesh’ are Ishmael’s children—slaves. Those born ‘according to the promise’ are Isaac’s children—free. The present Jerusalem corresponds to Hagar and Sinai—a devastating identification.

The Galatian believers are ‘children of promise, like Isaac.’ The conclusion is clear: the slave woman’s son will never share in the inheritance. The believer’s identity is that of the free woman’s child.

Calvin defends Paul’s allegorical method. The typological correspondence is grounded in history: Paul is not inventing meaning but discerning the pattern God wove into the narrative. Those who seek justification through the law are Hagar’s children; those who receive it by faith are Sarah’s children. The command to ‘cast out the slave woman’ applies to the false teaching, not to persons.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb allēgoreoumena is a present passive participle. The NET translators note that Paul’s method is more properly called typology than pure allegory: unlike Philo, Paul presupposes and builds on the historical narrative.
The phrase hē anō Ierousalēm draws on Jewish apocalyptic traditions of a heavenly Jerusalem (cf. 2 Bar 4:2–6; Rev 21:2).

moo.source: Douglas J. Moo, Galatians, BECNT (2013) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo argues the allegory is the capstone of the scriptural argument (chs. 3–4). The irony: the Judaizers appeal to Abraham’s circumcision, but Paul appeals to Abraham’s two sons and shows that the law-based approach corresponds to the slave woman’s line. Verse 31 concludes the entire doctrinal section: ‘we are not children of the slave woman, but of the free woman.’

bruce.source: F.F. Bruce, Galatians, NIGTC (1982) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Bruce compares Paul’s method to Philo’s treatment of the same narrative. Unlike Philo’s timeless philosophical extraction, Paul’s allegory is salvation-historical: it maps the Hagar-Sarah contrast onto the eschatological transition from old covenant to new. The geographical identification of Hagar with Mount Sinai in Arabia is textually complex but rhetorically effective.

hist.context: Paul’s most daring exegetical move. He takes the Judaizers’ own hero—Abraham—and turns the narrative against them. Abraham had two sons: Ishmael, born of the slave woman according to the flesh, and Isaac, born of the free woman according to the promise. Hagar corresponds to the Sinai covenant and present Jerusalem (in slavery with her children); Sarah corresponds to the covenant of promise and the Jerusalem above (free). The allegory concludes with the command to ‘cast out the slave woman and her son’ (Gen 21:10, v. 30), applied to the expulsion of the Judaizing teaching.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Inheritance analogy: the minor heir is no different from a slave

rows.label: vv.3–5
rows.text: Enslaved under stoicheia; fullness of time; God sent his Son

rows.label: vv.6–7
rows.text: Spirit of adoption: Abba, Father; heir through God

rows.label: vv.8–11
rows.text: Rebuke: turning back to weak and miserable forces

rows.label: vv.12–16
rows.text: Personal appeal: remember your former devotion

rows.label: vv.17–20
rows.text: Agitators’ motives; Paul’s birth-pangs for the Galatians

rows.label: vv.21–27
rows.text: Allegory of Hagar and Sarah: two covenants, two Jerusalems

rows.label: vv.28–31
rows.text: Children of promise: cast out the slave woman

lit.note: Galatians 4 moves from Christological exposition (vv. 1–7) through pastoral appeal (vv. 8–20) to allegorical argument (vv. 21–31). The central affirmation is the incarnation passage (vv. 4–5), which grounds adoption in God’s sending of his Son. The Hagar-Sarah allegory brings the doctrinal argument of chs. 1–4 to its climax.

themes.note: Covenant and Sovereignty share the highest scores as the chapter centres on God’s sovereign initiative in the incarnation and the covenant distinction between Hagar and Sarah. Prophecy is elevated by the fulfilment motifs: the ‘fullness of time’ and the eschatological ‘Jerusalem above.’

hebtext.note: A distinctively Pauline term (Rom 8:15, 23; 9:4; Eph 1:5) denoting the legal act by which a person is brought into a family with full rights of inheritance. In Roman law, adoption conferred the same status as biological sonship. Believers’ status as God’s children is not natural but gracious—an act of divine initiative.

One of the most debated expressions in Galatians. It may refer to basic principles of religious knowledge, elemental spirits, or the physical elements. Paul applies the term to both Jews (under the law) and Gentiles (under paganism) before Christ, suggesting that both systems function as enslavement to sub-Christian spiritual realities.

Paul describes the stoicheia as ‘weak and miserable’ (asthenē kai ptōcha, v. 9). The adjectives denote impotence: the elemental forces lack the power to bring about the righteousness they demand. To return to them is a step backward from power to impotence.

The verb appears only here in the NT. Paul explicitly identifies his interpretive method: the Genesis narrative is read typologically, with Hagar = Sinai covenant = slavery = present Jerusalem and Sarah = promise covenant = freedom = Jerusalem above. This is not arbitrary allegory but typological correspondence grounded in historical pattern.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

thread.text: Paul’s fuller exposition of adoption parallels Gal 4:4–7: those led by the Spirit are God’s children, crying ‘Abba, Father,’ and are co-heirs with Christ.
The incarnational language—‘the Word became flesh’—provides the Johannine parallel to Paul’s compressed Christology in Gal 4:4.
Paul’s warning against judging regarding festivals, New Moons, or Sabbath days parallels Gal 4:10. Both treat calendar observance as a return to the shadow superseded by Christ.
Paul’s tender self-description as a nursing mother parallels the parental imagery of Gal 4:19.
The original narrative of Ishmael’s expulsion provides the historical basis for Paul’s typological application.
Paul quotes Isaiah’s barren-woman oracle in v. 27, applying it to the Jerusalem above: the barren Sarah will have more children than Hagar.

discourse.thesis: The Galatians' return to law-observance is a regression from sonship to slavery — they must stand in the freedom that is theirs as full heirs through the Spirit.

nodes.text: The heir/slave analogy: An heir who is a child is no different from a slave despite owning everything. So we, when we were children, were enslaved under the elemental principles (στοιχεῖα) of the world. But when the fullness of time (τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ χρόνου) came, God sent his Son — born of woman, born under the law — to redeem those under the law so we might receive adoption as sons (υἱοθεσία).

children.text: Because you are sons, God sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying 'Abba! Father!' So you are no longer a slave but a son — and if a son, an heir through God. The argument from present spiritual experience confirms the theological conclusion.

nodes.text: Formerly the Galatians were enslaved to gods that by nature are not gods. Now, having known God (or rather, been known by God), how can they turn back to the weak and worthless elemental principles to be enslaved all over again? Paul fears his labor among them was wasted.

children.text: They are observing days and months and seasons and years — Jewish calendar observances that signal a reversion to pre-Christ religious bondage.

nodes.marker: Brothers, I beg you (Γίνεσθε ὡς ἐγώ)
nodes.text: Paul's personal appeal: Become as I am, for I became as you are. He reminds them of their original reception of him despite his physical affliction — they would have given their eyes for him. What happened? Has Paul become their enemy by telling the truth? The agitators court the Galatians for bad motives — they want to shut them out.

children.text: It is good to be zealously sought after in a good purpose — not only when Paul is present. The agitators' zeal is not good: they want to exclude the Galatians so that they will seek the agitators.
Paul is in anguish like a mother in labor until Christ is formed (μορφωθῇ) in them. He wishes he could change his tone — his perplexity is genuine.

nodes.text: The Sarah/Hagar allegory: those who want to be under the law, do they not hear the law? Abraham had two sons — one by the slave woman (Hagar/Sinai/present Jerusalem/bondage) and one by the free woman (Sarah/covenant of promise/Jerusalem above/freedom). The allegory maps: Ishmael = the agitators; Isaac = the Galatian believers.

children.text: As then the son born according to the flesh persecuted the son born according to the Spirit — so it is now. But what does Scripture say? Cast out the slave woman and her son. The heir of the free woman does not share inheritance with the son of the slave.
Therefore, brothers, we are not children of the slave woman but of the free woman. This single sentence prepares the transition to Chapter 5's call to freedom.

discourse.note: Galatians 4 moves through three modes of persuasion: theological argument (vv. 1-11), emotional appeal (vv. 12-20), and allegorical exegesis (vv. 21-31). Paul's anguish in vv. 19-20 is the emotional heart of the letter — the only place where he abandons argument and pleads as a mother. The Hagar/Sarah allegory is typological rather than allegorical in the modern sense — Paul reads Israel's history as pointing forward to its fulfillment in Christ.

coaching.tip: God sent the Spirit 'crying Abba, Father' (v. 6). The Spirit's first work isn't power or gifts—it's intimacy. 'Abba' is the child's term for father, shockingly familiar in prayer. Adoption isn't legal fiction; it rewires the believer's relational instincts toward the God who was once distant.

The Hagar-Sarah allegory (vv. 21-31) reads Genesis through eschatological lenses: Sinai corresponds to slavery, Jerusalem above to freedom. Paul's hermeneutic is christocentric—he reads the Old Testament backward from Christ. The two covenants produce two kinds of children; the question is which mother claims you.
